papergraph.title: In-Situ/Operando Characterization Techniques for Rechargeable Batteries: Electrodes and Electrolytes Origins
papergraph.citation: M. Dorri et al. (2025) In-Situ/Operando Characterization Techniques for Rechargeable Batteries: Electrodes and Electrolytes Origins doi:10.1149/ma2025-015606mtgabs
papergraph.abstract: Rechargeable batteries are essential to satisfy the increasing needs generated by electric vehicles, portable devices, and large-scale energy storage systems. A comprehensive understanding of the realistic operating conditions in batteries is necessary for performance, safety, and cycle life enhancement. In-situ/operando characterization techniques are currently essential instruments to investigate the structural, chemical, and morphological changes of electrode and electrolyte materials in real-time under actual operating conditions. Here, recent advancements in using in-situ/operando techniques to emphasize the origins and behaviors of electrodes and electrolytes in metal-ion, solid-state, and aqueous batteries are examined. State-of-the-art techniques offer significant advantages in battery research. In-situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) is utilized to explore phase transition, crystal structure evolution, and degradation mechanisms of electrodes. In-situ electron microscopy techniques like scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) enable direct visualization of electrode morphology changes, Li dendrite formation, and composition transformations at the Micro to atomic scale. These imaging techniques allow for detecting defects or cracks within the active material particles. In-situ atomic force microscopy (AFM) offers valuable information on the interface, such as mechanical properties, SEI morphology, and the growth of Li dendrite. In-situ neutron-related methods unravel lithium distribution and transport path in electrode materials. Additionally, the application of in-situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), which has been used to elucidate redox reactions, electronic structure changes, and local atomic arrangements, is discussed. Critical challenges are highlighted, including the necessity of improving spatial and temporal resolution, the complexity of interpreting data from multi-component systems, and the compatibility of techniques with commercial cells. Opportunities are also explored for integrating multiple in-situ/operando techniques, developing a stable SEI layer, and achieving reversible Li plating. The experimental setup and working principles are comprehensively explained. This work guides future research directions for next-generation rechargeable batteries by providing a detailed understanding of the fundamental processes at the electrode and electrolyte levels.

papergraph.title: Spatial and Temporal Analysis of Sodium-Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Dewen Hou et al. (2021) Spatial and Temporal Analysis of Sodium-Ion Batteries doi:10.1021/acsenergylett.1c01868
papergraph.abstract: As a promising alternative to the market-leading lithium-ion batteries, low-cost sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are attractive for applications such as large-scale electrical energy storage systems. The energy density, cycling life, and rate performance of SIBs are fundamentally dependent on dynamic physiochemical reactions, structural change, and morphological evolution. Therefore, it is essential to holistically understand SIBs reaction processes, degradation mechanisms, and thermal/mechanical behaviors in complex working environments. The recent developments of advanced in situ and operando characterization enable the establishment of the structure–processing–property–performance relationship in SIBs under operating conditions. This Review summarizes significant recent progress in SIBs exploiting in situ and operando techniques based on X-ray and electron analyses at different time and length scales. Through the combination of spectroscopy, imaging, and diffraction, local and global changes in SIBs can be elucidated for improving materials design. The fundamental principles and state-of-the-art capabilities of different techniques are presented, followed by elaborative discussions of major challenges and perspectives.

papergraph.title: Improvement of Environmental Sustainability and Circular Economy through Construction Waste Management for Material Reuse
papergraph.citation: Stefan Schützenhofer et al. (2022) Improvement of Environmental Sustainability and Circular Economy through Construction Waste Management for Material Reuse doi:10.3390/su141711087
papergraph.abstract: The Architecture, Engineering, and Construction industries are allocated 40–60% of the worldwide raw material extraction. Construction waste accounts for a significant share of the total waste volume. Therefore, careless handling reduces natural resources and waste deposits (landfills). Furthermore, material reuse and recycling can reduce resource and energy consumption and environmental emissions in some cases. Waste management concepts in the fields of Architecture, Engineering, and Construction are increasingly in the European Union and worldwide focus. A circular economy can be seen as a system in which resource input, waste, emission, and energy leakage are minimised due to closed material loops. Therefore, implementing a consistent Circular Economic requires a holistic approach in which material, emissions, and energy are put into context. This paper aims to analyse dismantling, recovery, and recycling processes and link relevant parameters to assess material sustainability. The technical effort must be made, and the associated costs are compared with the influence of eco-indicators. Furthermore, the data required can be used for the following three areas: Facilitating demolition planning and on-site waste management; resource management at the local/regional/state level; and governmental tax mechanisms.

papergraph.title: Integrating Reverse Logistics and Life Cycle Assessment for Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling: Advancing Circular Economy and Environmental Sustainability in Indonesia’s Electric Vehicle Industry
papergraph.citation: Isna Nugraha et al. (2026) Integrating Reverse Logistics and Life Cycle Assessment for Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling: Advancing Circular Economy and Environmental Sustainability in Indonesia’s Electric Vehicle Industry doi:10.11594/nstp.2026.5431
papergraph.abstract: The increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) presents both significant opportunities and challenges, particularly in the management of end-of-life (EOL) lithium-ion batteries. These batteries, which power EVs, contain valuable and hazardous materials, making their disposal and recycling crucial for environmental sustainability. This study explores the integration of Reverse Logistics (RL) and Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to optimize the recycling process of lithium-ion batteries, with a strong focus on supporting circular economy principles. The research highlights the critical role of RL in the efficient collection, transportation, and processing of used batteries, while LCA provides a comprehensive evaluation of the environmental impacts associated with battery recycling compared to the production of new batteries. Key challenges identified include infrastructure limitations, safety concerns regarding battery reuse, and a lack of consumer trust in the recycling process. By integrating RL and LCA, this study aims to address these challenges, demonstrating how RL can streamline supply chains, reduce costs, and improve recycling efficiency. Furthermore, the study underscores the importance of policy frameworks and technological advancements in enhancing the effectiveness of reverse logistics systems within the electric vehicle battery recycling sector. The findings suggest that strengthening governmental policies, improving infrastructure, and fostering consumer participation are critical to establishing a sustainable and economically viable battery recycling industry. By integrating RL and LCA, this study emphasizes the potential for contributing to a greener, more circular economy in Indonesia’s electric vehicle sector, paving the way for a more sustainable future.

papergraph.title: Recovery of Metals from Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Green Solvents: A Sustainable Approach to Reducing Waste and Environmental Impact
papergraph.citation: Katherine Moreno et al. (2025) Recovery of Metals from Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Green Solvents: A Sustainable Approach to Reducing Waste and Environmental Impact doi:10.3390/recycling10060218
papergraph.abstract: The recovery of critical metals from spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is essential to reduce environmental impacts and promote circular economy strategies. This study developed a sustainable and scalable process for the recovery and complete valorization of lithium, cobalt, and other valuable components from end-of-life LIBs. Hydrometallurgical treatment using biodegradable citric and oxalic acids was employed as a green alternative to conventional inorganic acids, achieving high selectivity and reduced environmental impact. Experimental work was conducted on 3 kg of LIBs from discarded laptop batteries (Dell and HP). After safe discharge and dismantling, the cathode materials were thermally treated at 300 °C to detach active components, followed by acid leaching in 1 M citric acid at 30 °C, pH 2.5, and 6 h of reaction. Lithium and cobalt were recovered as oxalates with efficiencies of 90% and 85%, respectively, while copper, aluminum, and graphite were separated through mechanical and thermal processes. Beyond metal recovery, the process demonstrates a circular upcycling approach, transforming recovered materials into functional products such as aluminum keychains, copper jewelry, and graphite-based pencils. This integrated strategy connects hydrometallurgical extraction with material reuse, advancing toward a zero-waste, closed-loop system for sustainable LIB recycling and local resource valorization.

papergraph.title: Advances in Recycling and Resource Recovery of Post-Consumer Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) Waste for Sustainable Waste Management and Circular Economy
papergraph.citation: (2025) Advances in Recycling and Resource Recovery of Post-Consumer Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) Waste for Sustainable Waste Management and Circular Economy doi:10.25163/energy.3110048
papergraph.abstract: Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is widely used in textiles, packaging, and consumer goods; however, its extensive consumption has led to significant environmental challenges due to post-consumer waste accumulation. This review explores recent advancements in PET recycling, focusing on chemical and enzymatic methods as sustainable alternatives to traditional mechanical recycling. Chemical recycling processes, including glycolysis, methanolysis, and hydrolysis, break PET into monomers, enabling the production of high-quality recycled materials. Enzymatic recycling, utilizing PET-degrading enzymes, offers an eco-friendly approach with mild reaction conditions and high specificity. The review compares these methods in terms of efficiency, scalability, energy requirements, and life cycle assessments (LCAs), highlighting enzymatic recycling’s lower environmental impact. Additionally, economic feasibility and large-scale implementation challenges, including contamination and cost-effectiveness, are discussed. Despite technological advancements, further innovation and policy support are essential to optimize these recycling strategies, foster a circular economy, and mitigate PET-related environmental pollution.

papergraph.title: A review on recent environmental electrochemistry approaches for the consolidation of a circular economy model
papergraph.citation: Gustavo Acosta-Santoyo et al. (2023) A review on recent environmental electrochemistry approaches for the consolidation of a circular economy model. doi:10.1016/j.chemosphere.2023.140573
papergraph.abstract: Availability of raw materials in the chemical industry is related to the selection of the chemical processes in which they are used as well as to the efficiency, cost, and eventual evolution to more competitive dynamics of transformation technologies. In general terms however, any chemically transforming technology starts with the extraction, purification, design, manufacture, use, and disposal of materials. It is important to create a new paradigm towards green chemistry, sustainability, and circular economy in the chemical sciences that help to better employ, reuse, and recycle the materials used in every aspect of modern life. Electrochemistry is a growing field of knowledge that can help with these issues to reduce solid waste and the impact of chemical processes on the environment. Several electrochemical studies in the last decades have benefited the recovery of important chemical compounds and elements through electrodeposition, electrowinning, electrocoagulation, electrodialysis, and other processes. The use of living organisms and microorganisms using an electrochemical perspective (known as bioelectrochemistry), is also calling attention to 'mining', through plants and microorganisms, essential chemical elements. New process design or the optimization of the current technologies is a major necessity to enhance production and minimize the use of raw materials along with less generation of wastes and secondary by-products. In this context, this contribution aims to show an up-to-date scenario of both environmental electrochemical and bioelectrochemical processes for the extraction, use, recovery and recycling of materials in a circular economy model.

papergraph.title: Advancements and Challenges in Lithium-Ion Battery Lifecycle Management Toward a Sustainable Circular Economy for Electric Vehicles
papergraph.citation: Sureshkumar Alagarsamy et al. (2025) Advancements and Challenges in Lithium-Ion Battery Lifecycle Management Toward a Sustainable Circular Economy for Electric Vehicles doi:10.1109/OJPEL.2025.3604475
papergraph.venue: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
papergraph.abstract: The increasing global shift towards electric vehicles (EVs) has led to a surge in demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), which serve as the primary energy storage solution for modern EVs. However, with this growth comes significant challenges related to battery performance, sustainability, and end-of-life (EOL) management. This article provides a comprehensive review of battery technologies, focusing on advancements in lithium-ion batteries, their degradation mechanisms, and the environmental impact of battery disposal. Additionally, it examines the challenges in EV battery recycling, including safety hazards, disassembly complexities, and economic feasibility. The study explores second-life applications of retired EV batteries in energy storage systems, microgrids, and hybrid renewable energy solutions, emphasizing their potential to extend battery usability while minimizing waste. Furthermore, advanced screening, refurbishment, and material recovery techniques are analyzed to improve resource efficiency and reduce the need for new raw material extraction. By proposing a systematic framework for sustainable battery lifecycle management, this research aims to promote a circular economy, optimize battery reusability, and support the transition towards a cleaner, more sustainable future in electromobility.

papergraph.title: An economic analysis of residential photovoltaic systems with lithium ion battery storage in the United States
papergraph.citation: Eric J. Tervo et al. (2018) An economic analysis of residential photovoltaic systems with lithium ion battery storage in the United States doi:10.1016/J.RSER.2018.06.055
papergraph.abstract: Residential photovoltaic systems can reduce reliance on grid electricity, which may be desirable for numerous reasons. However, the economic viability of such systems is dependent on effective use of excess electricity generation, most often through net or bi-directional metering. With recent cost reductions in residential-scale lithium ion battery storage systems, these may be a practical alternative. In this work, we model the lifetime performance and economics of residential photovoltaics with lithium ion batteries across all 50 states in the U.S. We investigate in detail how photovoltaic size and battery capacity affect performance and cost metrics in California, Georgia, and Massachusetts. We find that with appropriate sizing, photovoltaic-battery systems can be more affordable than photovoltaics alone. We demonstrate that these systems may be competitive with grid prices when the federal investment tax credit and favorable financing terms are used, and we calculate the sell-back price required for bi-directional metering to reach cost parity with photovoltaic-battery systems in every state.

papergraph.title: Advanced Air Mobility: Opportunities and Challenges Deploying eVTOLs for Air Ambulance Service
papergraph.citation: R. Goyal et al. (2022) Advanced Air Mobility: Opportunities and Challenges Deploying eVTOLs for Air Ambulance Service doi:10.3390/app12031183
papergraph.abstract: Advanced air mobility (AAM) is a broad concept enabling consumers access to on-demand air mobility, cargo and package delivery, healthcare applications, and emergency services through an integrated and connected multimodal transportation network. While a number of technical and social concerns have been raised about AAM, early use cases for emergency response and aeromedical transport may be key to demonstrating the concept and building public acceptance. Using a five-step multi-method approach consisting of preliminary scoping, modeling performance metrics, developing baseline assumptions, analyzing scenarios, and applying a Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis, this study examines the potential operational and market viability of the air ambulance market using a variety of aircraft and propulsion types. The analysis concludes that electric vertical take-off and land (eVTOL) aircraft could confront a number of operational and economic challenges for aeromedical applications compared to hybrid vertical take-off and land (VTOL) aircraft and rotorcraft. The study finds that technological improvements such as reduced charge times, increased operational range, and battery swapping could make the eVTOL aircraft more reliable and cost-effective for aeromedical transport.

papergraph.title: Battery revenues in energy and balancing markets for different battery technologies using FASBATT™
papergraph.citation: César Alberte Tapia et al. (2026) Battery revenues in energy and balancing markets for different battery technologies using FASBATT™ doi:10.1088/1755-1315/1588/1/012005
papergraph.venue: IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environment
papergraph.abstract: The increasing integration of renewable generation in power grids and the transition away from fossil-fuel generation bring the need for widespread installation of stationary electricity storage to provide power systems with more flexibility. Pumped hydroelectric storage has been the predominant grid-level storage technology until now, but the flexibility, modularity, and cost of battery storage technologies make them the clear choice for the transition to power systems based on renewable generation. Battery energy storage systems (BESS) are projected to lead the growth of installed storage capacity in the short-term and long-term future, becoming the predominant storage technology before 2030. A literature review and BESS industry analysis reveals the position of lithium-ion batteries, specifically its lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) chemistry, as the primary technology for stationary storage applications, aided by their dominance in portable electronics and electric vehicle markets. However, several issues with lithium-ion batteries show the benefits of diversifying the battery market, and the wide range of applications in stationary energy storage may contain opportunities for new technologies, such as vanadium redox flow batteries, sodium-ion batteries or iron-air batteries, to compete or coexist with LFP batteries. The market for these technologies is still small, and market opportunities are uncertain, but their sustainability and potential cost effectiveness make them attractive alternatives. The battery industry is recent and constantly evolving, with continuous advances and the emergence of new technologies. This contributes to the heterogeneity of the industry and the uncertainty and disparity in the modeling of some battery behaviors, which causes difficulties in dispatch planning and economic viability analysis of different business models. BESS simulations with different technology profiles reveal that improvements in BESS costs and performance are needed for standalone BESS projects to become feasible based on the price arbitrage in the day-ahead spot market. The secondary frequency regulation market offers more attractive opportunities for the moment.

papergraph.title: Techno-economics of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes for augmented reality displays and data communication applications
papergraph.citation: Khaled Ahmed (2025) Techno-economics of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes for augmented reality displays and data communication applications doi:10.1117/12.3045931
papergraph.abstract: This paper introduces a concept to the growth of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes (a-NWLEDs) using metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD), addressing long-standing concerns about the scalability and cost-effectiveness of molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) for high-volume manufacturing. a-NWLEDs offer significant promise for next-generation technologies such as augmented reality displays and chip-to-chip data communication due to their potential for high brightness, directional emission, and compatibility with silicon-based CMOS platforms. To evaluate the feasibility of MOCVD as an alternative growth method, a comprehensive techno-economic analysis compares MBE and MOCVD across two critical performance indicators: MicroLED wafer production cost and capital investment required to meet market demand. The comparison is rooted in the details of the epitaxial growth technology. The results demonstrate that MOCVD significantly outperforms MBE in both metrics, providing a scalable and cost-effective solution for manufacturing a-NWLEDs. These findings underscore the potential of MOCVD to enable the widespread commercialization of a-NWLEDs, offering a viable pathway for meeting the performance and economic requirements of advanced display and communication applications.

papergraph.title: Techno-Economic Analysis of CO2 Storage in Depleted Oil and Gas Reservoirs, Saline Aquifers, and EOR
papergraph.citation: K. Enab et al. (2024) Techno-Economic Analysis of CO2 Storage in Depleted Oil and Gas Reservoirs, Saline Aquifers, and EOR doi:10.2118/220955-ms
papergraph.venue: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition
papergraph.abstract: Addressing climate change urgently requires effective carbon capture and storage (CCS) strategies to lower atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) levels. Depleted oil and gas reservoirs and saline aquifers are promising for CO2 geological sequestration due to their significant storage capacities. Furthermore, CO2 injection for Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) is a potential storage option for CO2 when CO2 flow back is controlled. Therefore, this study provides a techno-economic analysis to evaluate the feasibility, efficiency, and economic analysis of these different geological storage options for CO2 storage. The economic evaluations were conducted in compliance with Section 45Q tax credits for financial viability. The analysis employs a multidisciplinary methodology combining geological assessments, engineering principles, and economic models. It focuses on the long-term impact of injecting CO2. Additionally, it evaluates supercritical CO2 behavior to estimate potential trapping mechanisms and identify factors affecting sequestration efficiency and safety. Economic analysis is central to this study, detailing the costs associated with CO2 capture, compression, transportation, injection, and monitoring. The study also considers the influence of policies, regulations, and market conditions on CCS project economics, identifying incentives and barriers. The findings of this study affirm the potential of depleted oil and gas reservoirs and saline aquifers as viable CO2 storage solutions, offering a nuanced understanding of their role in global carbon mitigation efforts. The results show the economic superiority of the depleted oil and gas reservoirs in storing CO2 if the storage capacity is ignored (i.e., if all storing options are capable of storing the desired CO2 — 1 MM metric tons in this study). The results highlight that storing CO2 in depleted oil and gas reservoirs exhibits the highest financial viability, particularly when used in conjunction with Direct Air Capture (DAC) technologies. These options demonstrate the greatest Net Present Values (NPVs), making them attractive for large-scale CO2 storage projects. Compliant DAC facilities, particularly those utilizing depleted oil and gas reservoirs, achieve NPVs upwards of $27 million, underscoring their economic superiority. While saline aquifers and EOR present viable options due to higher storing capacity, their financial performance is generally lower compared to depleted oil and gas reservoirs if uniform stored volume is considered. The study also notes the significant cost variations influenced by factors such as CO2 capture, transportation, and storage technologies, alongside the availability of financial incentives. By outlining the challenges and opportunities, the study provides essential insights for stakeholders in CCS projects, suggesting a pathway through the complexities of CO2 sequestration towards sustainable climate action. Compliance with regulatory standards, particularly the Section 45Q tax credits, is emphasized as crucial for achieving positive financial outcomes and ensuring the success of CCS initiatives.

papergraph.title: Preventing Crossover in Redox Flow Batteries through Active Material Oligomerization
papergraph.citation: S. Odom (2018) Preventing Crossover in Redox Flow Batteries through Active Material Oligomerization doi:10.1021/acscentsci.8b00099
papergraph.abstract: The ability to store large quantities of energy generated by renewable, but intermittent, sources like solar or wind in systems integrated within the country’s electrical grid is critical to the reduction of CO2 emissions and slowing the rate of global warming. Among the energy storage technologies under consideration, redox flow batteries are promising candidates. While large-scale installations of vanadium-containing flow batteries have been utilized in Japan and China, the widespread adoption of these batteries for grid storage purposes has been limited due to economics, safety concerns, and limited bankability. New electrolytes and longer-running installations have begun to mitigate concerns and have proven particularly useful in remote locationsespecially island communities and those in which the grid is less reliableas well as in connection to municipal grids in the United States (e.g., WA, MA), many of which are cofunded by the DOE’s Office of Electricity Delivery and Energy Reliability. Redox flow batteries consist of a few major components: tanks of charged (or to-be-charged) electrolytes, electronics to do the charging (discharging), membranes to separate the electrolyte solutions, and pumps to move liquids around. In commercial vanadium-based species, systems installed by Japan-based Sumitomo Electric Industries are still successfully in operation decades in, but the cost of materials limits overall economic viability. Replacing current vanadium-based electrolytes with those containing organic redox materials is thought by many to be a route to obtaining more commercially competitive flow batteries. While some groups are focused on organics in aqueous solutions of various pHs, others have explored nonaqueous electrolytes. In the latter case, a major advantage stems from the increase in the stability window of aprotic, nonaqueous electrolytes, which may be about 3 times (possibly 4 times) greater than that of aqueous solutions. The result would beif all other performance metrics are equalsmaller footprint batteries. Given the massive sizes of these batteries, realizing smaller footprints would greatly assist in their ease in installation in existing communities. To provide an example, the company UniEnergy advertises that “a compact footprint of only five 20’ containers” are required to deliver 600 kW of power and 2.2 MWh maximum energy (Figure 1). The bane of many a flow battery chemist’s existence is the performance of the membrane that separates the positive and negative solutions, called the posolyte (or catholyte) and negolyte (or anolyte). Separating the components of these solutions from one another is critical to keeping charged components (and their neutral versions) in the appropriate tanks. If species crossover occurs, capacity losses of 50% are inevitable. In acidic aqueous flow batteries, membranesalbeit expensiveexist for keeping species in their respective tanks, but for other pHs and for nonaqueous electrolytes, membranes are still in development. Researchers can choose to wait for better membranes, work on developing better membranes, orin the case of Hendriks et al.can tailor the electrolyte for a size exclusion approach. In the latest work from the Sanford group at the University of Michigan, with collaborators Helms, Sigman, and Minteer, a dual approach is reported: the combination of an oligomeric posolyte (or catholyte) with a membrane composed of polymers exhibiting intrinsic microporosity (PIMs). In their recent publication in ACS Central Science, Hendriks et al. report the synthesis and characterization of tris(dialkylamino)cyclopropenium oligomers, some of which are represented in the chemical drawing below. The oligomers were prepared to evaluate their performance with size exclusion membranes composed of PIMs. By increasing the molecular weight of the oligomers in tandem with decreasing membrane pore size through cross-linking, the authors hoped to minimize membrane crossover of this active species. A recent publication by coauthor Helms showed that the PIMs limited the crossover of other oligomers containing redox-active viologen,

papergraph.title: A Review on Technological Advancement in Charge Storage Technologies for Renewable Energy Applications
papergraph.citation: O. Nwandieze et al. (2026) A Review on Technological Advancement in Charge Storage Technologies for Renewable Energy Applications. doi:10.51244/ijrsi.2026.1304000123
papergraph.venue: International journal of research and scientific innovation
papergraph.abstract: The intermittent nature of solar and wind power necessitates reliable energy storage to stabilize electrical grids and enable high renewable penetration. This review provides a systematic assessment of charge storage technologies for renewable energy applications, with emphasis on both conventional systems and emerging post lithium solutions. A structured literature search was conducted across Scopus, Web of Science, Google Scholar, ScienceDirect, and IEEE Xplore for the period January 2013 to March 2026, yielding 101 peer reviewed sources after screening. Technologies are analyzed according to their operating mechanisms including electrochemical, electrostatic, mechanical, chemical, and thermal, as well as performance metrics such as energy density, power density, cycle life, and efficiency, and application suitability including grid scale systems, transport, and portable electronics. Key findings include the following: sodium ion batteries have emerged as compelling alternatives to lithium ion batteries for stationary storage due to sodium abundance and manufacturing compatibility, though energy density remains lower; solid state batteries offer intrinsic safety and high volumetric energy but face persistent electrode electrolyte interface challenges; advanced nanostructured materials particularly graphene, MXenes, and silicon based anodes enable hybrid systems that bridge the energy power gap between batteries and supercapacitors; no single technology satisfies all grid requirements, instead portfolios combining fast response supercapacitors for power quality, batteries for hourly shifting, hydrogen for seasonal storage, and thermal storage for concentrated solar power plants are necessary. Limitations of this review include the lack of quantitative meta analysis due to heterogeneous reporting standards. Future research priorities should focus on earth abundant materials, durable solid state interfaces, scalable recycling processes, and hybrid system integration. Charge storage technologies remain a fundamental prerequisite for a renewable powered future.

keynote.core_problem: The development of sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) as a cost-effective and high-performance alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for large-scale energy storage, while addressing challenges such as irreversible phase transformations, poor air stability, complex charge-compensation mechanisms, and relatively high costs.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) are ideal alternatives for large-scale energy storage systems due to abundant sodium resources and the use of inexpensive, noncritical transition metals.
important_claims.evidence: Sodium resources are abundantly distributed in the earth’s crust, and high-performance NIB cathode materials can be fabricated using solely inexpensive and noncritical transition metals such as manganese and iron, which further reduces the cost of the required raw materials.

important_claims.claim: Sodium layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2) have the potential to reduce costs, increase energy density and cycling stability, and improve the safety of NIBs for large-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: NaxTMO2 represents an important family of cathode materials with these potential benefits, though they face several key challenges.

keynotes.title: Comparative Techno-Economic and Life Cycle Assessment of Stationary Energy Storage Systems: Lithium-Ion, Lead-Acid, and Hydrogen

keynote.title: Comparative Techno-Economic and Life Cycle Assessment of Stationary Energy Storage Systems: Lithium-Ion, Lead-Acid, and Hydrogen
keynote.core_problem: Evaluating the techno-economic and environmental performance of different stationary energy storage technologies to guide decision-making for decarbonization and sustainability.
contributions: A comparative analysis of lithium-ion batteries, lead-acid batteries, and hydrogen systems using LCA and LCOS.
Holistic evaluation integrating capital and operational expenditures, efficiency, and cycling frequency.
methods: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) covering cradle-to-grave stages.
Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS) accounting for CAPEX, OPEX, efficiency, and cycling frequency.
experiments_or_evidence: Lithium-ion batteries achieve the lowest LCOS (120–180 EUR/MWh) and high round-trip efficiency (90–95%).
Lead-acid batteries have low CAPEX and high recyclability (>95%) but limited cycle life and lower efficiency (75–80%).
Hydrogen systems are costly (>250 EUR/MWh) and less efficient (30–40%), but advantageous for long-term and seasonal storage.

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to explore the potential of 'green' hydrogen production and its impact on the cost and efficiency of hydrogen systems.

keynote.core_problem: The need for next-generation secondary batteries to address the limitations of conventional lithium-ion batteries, such as higher energy density, enhanced safety, and improved supply chain sustainability.
contributions: Provides a comprehensive overview of recent trends in policy, market, and technology related to next-generation secondary batteries from a commercialization-oriented perspective.

gap_signals.text: Outlines future research and development directions toward the large-scale commercialization of next-generation secondary batteries.

important_claims.claim: Next-generation secondary batteries have the potential for higher energy density, enhanced safety, and improved supply chain sustainability compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries.
important_claims.evidence: The review discusses key technological challenges and representative approaches for each type of next-generation battery system, such as interfacial stability, safety, cycle life, and manufacturability.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review offers valuable insights into the current status and future prospects of various next-generation secondary battery technologies, aiding researchers and industry stakeholders in identifying critical areas for further investigation and investment.

keynotes.title: Designing Advanced Lithium‐Based Batteries for Low‐Temperature Conditions

keynote.title: Designing Advanced Lithium‐Based Batteries for Low‐Temperature Conditions
keynote.core_problem: Conventional lithium-ion batteries do not perform optimally under extremely low-temperature conditions, which is a critical issue for their use in electric vehicles, grid storage, space, defense, and subsea operations.
contributions: Overview of the challenges in developing lithium-ion batteries for low-temperature use.
Introduction of next-generation battery chemistries (lithium-metal, lithium-sulfur, and dual-ion) that may be better suited for low-temperature conditions.
limitations: The new chemistries (lithium-metal, lithium-sulfur, and dual-ion) also present unique challenges at low temperatures.

gap_signals.text: The new chemistries (lithium-metal, lithium-sulfur, and dual-ion) also present unique challenges at low temperatures.
These three chemistries are presented as prototypical examples, but more work is needed to fully understand and overcome their unique challenges at low temperatures.

important_claims.claim: Next-generation chemistries (lithium-metal, lithium-sulfur, and dual-ion) could overcome many of the low-temperature performance issues of conventional lithium-ion batteries.
important_claims.evidence: The article evaluates these chemistries as being potentially better suited for low-temperature conditions, though they still have their own unique challenges.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The paper provides a valuable starting point for researchers looking to develop or improve battery technologies for low-temperature applications, by highlighting the potential and challenges of different next-generation battery chemistries.

keynotes.title: A Review of Capacity Fade Mechanism and Promotion Strategies for Lithium Iron Phosphate Batteries

keynote.title: A Review of Capacity Fade Mechanism and Promotion Strategies for Lithium Iron Phosphate Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Capacity decline and poor low-temperature performance in lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries, which are caused by several factors including irreversible phase transition, formation of the cathode–electrolyte interface (CEI) layer, dissolution of iron elements, oxidative decomposition of the electrolyte, growth and thickening of the solid–electrolyte interface (SEI) film, structural deterioration of graphite anodes, and growth of lithium dendrites.
contributions: Summarizes the working principle, crystal structure, and electrochemical performance of LFP batteries.
Discusses the degradation mechanisms of LFP batteries from the perspectives of cathode material, anode material, and electrolyte.
Reviews the research status on LFP material modification and electrolyte design to improve battery performance.

gap_signals.text: The challenges and future development of LFP batteries are prospected.

important_claims.claim: LFP batteries have advantages in safety, stability, and low cost.
important_claims.evidence: Commercialized LFP batteries have become mainstream energy storage solutions.

important_claims.claim: Several factors contribute to the capacity fade and poor low-temperature performance of LFP batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Factors include irreversible phase transition, CEI layer formation, iron element dissolution, electrolyte oxidative decomposition, SEI film growth, graphite anode deterioration, and lithium dendrite growth.

important_claims.claim: Modification, doping, and coating of cathode materials, electrolyte design, and anode coating can improve the electrochemical performance of LFP batteries.
important_claims.evidence: These methods are studied to address the performance issues in LFP batteries.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a comprehensive review of the current understanding of LFP battery degradation mechanisms and strategies to enhance their performance, useful for researchers focusing on improving LFP battery technology.

keynote.core_problem: Understanding and addressing the primary causes of cathode failure in spent lithium-ion batteries, and the challenges in their direct regeneration for industrial application.
contributions: Analysis of the primary causes of failure in three representative battery cathodes (lithium iron phosphate, layered lithium transition metal oxide, and lithium cobalt oxide).
Review and summary of the latest research status on direct regeneration of spent lithium-ion batteries.
Introduction of application examples of direct regeneration technology in production practice and the problems exposed in the initial stage of industrialization.
methods: Theoretical analysis of cathode failure mechanisms.
Review of five direct regeneration methods: solid-state sintering, hydrothermal, eutectic molten salt, electrochemical, and chemical lithiation.
limitations: Problems exposed in the initial stage of industrialization of direct regeneration technology.

gap_signals.text: Problems exposed in the initial stage of industrialization of direct regeneration technology.
The problems exposed at the early stage of the industrialization of direct regeneration technology are revealed.
Efficient stripping of material from the collector to obtain pure cathode material has become a first challenge in recycling.

important_claims.claim: Direct regeneration method is widely concerned by researchers due to its advantages of in situ regeneration, short process, and less pollutant emission.
important_claims.evidence: Direct regeneration method has been widely concerned by researchers in the field of battery recycling because of its advantages of in situ regeneration, short process, and less pollutant emission.

Simulated the temperature environment during actual use to study the capacity characteristics of the batteries.

Analysis of discharge efficiencies and internal resistance over time and state of charge (SOC).
assumptions: The single battery is representative of the power battery's behavior in real-world conditions.

keynotes.title: A Polymerized‐Ionic‐Liquid‐Based Polymer Electrolyte with High Oxidative Stability for 4 and 5 V Class Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: A Polymerized‐Ionic‐Liquid‐Based Polymer Electrolyte with High Oxidative Stability for 4 and 5 V Class Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Polymer solid electrolytes for solid-state batteries typically suffer from low ionic conductivity and low oxidative stability.
contributions: Development of a polymer electrolyte based on a polymerized ionic liquid and an ionic liquid plasticizer
Achievement of high room-temperature ionic conductivity (0.8 mS cm−1) and high oxidative stability (>5.0 V versus Li+/Li)
Demonstration of compatibility with lithium metal and non-flammability upon direct flame exposure
Stable performance in symmetric lithium metal cells and with high-energy uncoated layered and high-voltage spinel cathodes
methods: Synthesis and characterization of the polymer electrolyte
Electrochemical testing in symmetric lithium metal cells
Cycling tests with high-energy uncoated layered LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 and high-voltage spinel LiMn1.5Ni0.5O4 cathodes
experiments_or_evidence: Achieved ionic conductivity of 0.8 mS cm−1 at room temperature
Confirmed oxidative stability >5.0 V versus Li+/Li
Stable lithium plating and stripping in symmetric cells for over 1700 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 per half cycle
Initial capacity of 162 mA h g−1 and 72% capacity retention after 600 cycles with LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 cathode to 4.4 V
Initial capacity of 132 mAh g−1 and 76% capacity retention after 300 cycles with LiMn1.5Ni0.5O4 cathode to 5 V

gap_signals.text: The high oxidative stability and technological potential of this electrolyte for high-voltage solid-state lithium metal batteries are confirmed, suggesting further research into practical applications and scaling.

important_claims.claim: The polymer electrolyte offers simultaneously high ionic conductivity and high oxidative stability.
important_claims.evidence: The electrolyte achieves a room-temperature ionic conductivity of 0.8 mS cm−1 and oxidative stability >5.0 V versus Li+/Li.

important_claims.claim: The electrolyte is compatible with lithium metal and is non-flammable.
important_claims.evidence: It enables stable lithium plating and stripping, and does not ignite when exposed to direct flame.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a new approach to developing polymer electrolytes with both high ionic conductivity and high oxidative stability, which can be used as a reference for future studies in solid-state lithium metal batteries, especially for high-voltage applications.

keynotes.title: New Oxyhalide Solid Electrolytes with High Lithium Ionic Conductivity >10 mS/cm for All-Solid-State Batteries

keynote.title: New Oxyhalide Solid Electrolytes with High Lithium Ionic Conductivity >10 mS/cm for All-Solid-State Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The ionic conductivities of halide solid electrolytes (SEs) are not as high as those of other SEs, particularly sulfides, limiting their use in all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs).
contributions: Discovery of new lithium-metal-oxy-halide materials, LiMOCl4 (M = Nb, Ta), with high ionic conductivity.
Demonstration of the potential of these oxyhalides as promising SE candidates for practical ASSB applications.
methods: Synthesis and testing of new lithium-metal-oxy-halide materials, LiMOCl4 (M = Nb, Ta).
experiments_or_evidence: Ionic conductivities of 10.4 mS/cm for M = Nb and 12.4 mS/cm for M = Ta in cold-pressed powder forms at room temperature.
Capacity retention of 80% at 5 C/0.1 C in bulk-type ASSB cells using the oxyhalides as the cathode SE.

gap_signals.text: The ionic conductivity is comparable to or surpasses that of organic liquid electrolytes, but further improvements may be necessary for broader commercial application.

datasets_or_materials: LiMOCl4 (M = Nb, Ta) as new lithium-metal-oxy-halide materials.

important_claims.claim: The ionic conductivities of the new oxyhalide materials are extremely high, even in cold-pressed powder forms at room temperature.
important_claims.evidence: Measured ionic conductivities of 10.4 mS/cm for M = Nb and 12.4 mS/cm for M = Ta.

important_claims.claim: Bulk-type ASSB cells using the oxyhalides as the cathode SE show an outstanding rate capability.
important_claims.evidence: Capacity retention of 80% at 5 C/0.1 C.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The discovery of these new oxyhalide materials can serve as a foundation for further research into high-conductivity solid electrolytes, potentially leading to more efficient and stable all-solid-state batteries.

keynotes.title: Evaluation and Characterization of SEI Composition in Lithium Metal and Anode‐Free Lithium Batteries

keynote.title: Evaluation and Characterization of SEI Composition in Lithium Metal and Anode‐Free Lithium Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The stability and performance of lithium metal and anode-free lithium batteries are significantly affected by the solid electrolyte interface (SEI), which can lead to issues such as dendrite growth, short circuits, and rapid capacity degradation if not properly managed.
contributions: Review of recent research on SEI formation in lithium-metal and anode-free lithium batteries
Discussion of stabilization strategies for SEI
Examination of new real-time characterization methods for SEI

gap_signals.text: Improving SEI stability is vital for enhancing battery performance, and researchers are exploring various strategies, such as the use of electrolyte additives and synthetic SEI films.

important_claims.claim: The structural properties of the SEI are related to lithium stripping and plating efficiency, as well as to the overall battery lifespan.
important_claims.evidence: The SEI, which is formed by electrolyte decomposition on the electrode surface, affects battery stability, electrochemistry, and cycle life.

important_claims.claim: In lithium metal batteries, the SEI must manage the significant volume changes of the electrode and prevent dendrite growth that can lead to short circuits and capacity losses.
important_claims.evidence: This challenge is exacerbated in anode‐free lithium batteries, where uncontrolled SEI growth can cause rapid capacity degradation.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the current understanding of SEI in lithium metal and anode-free lithium batteries, highlighting key areas for improvement and suggesting advanced characterization techniques. important_claims.evidence: By integrating RL and LCA, this study aims to address these challenges, demonstrating how RL can streamline supply chains, reduce costs, and improve recycling efficiency.

important_claims.claim: Strengthening governmental policies, improving infrastructure, and fostering consumer participation are critical to establishing a sustainable and economically viable battery recycling industry
important_claims.evidence: The findings suggest that strengthening governmental policies, improving infrastructure, and fostering consumer participation are critical to establishing a sustainable and economically viable battery recycling industry.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a framework for integrating RL and LCA in the context of lithium-ion battery recycling, which can be applied to other regions and industries to enhance circular economy principles and environmental sustainability.

keynotes.title: Recovery of Metals from Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Green Solvents: A Sustainable Approach to Reducing Waste and Environmental Impact

keynote.title: Recovery of Metals from Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Green Solvents: A Sustainable Approach to Reducing Waste and Environmental Impact
keynote.core_problem: The need for a sustainable and environmentally friendly method to recover critical metals from spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to reduce waste and promote a circular economy.
contributions: Developed a sustainable and scalable process for the recovery and valorization of lithium, cobalt, and other valuable components from end-of-life LIBs.
Utilized biodegradable citric and oxalic acids as green alternatives to conventional inorganic acids, achieving high selectivity and reduced environmental impact.
methods: Hydrometallurgical treatment using biodegradable citric and oxalic acids.
Thermal treatment at 300 °C to detach active components from cathode materials.
Acid leaching in 1 M citric acid at 30 °C, pH 2.5, for 6 hours.
experiments_or_evidence: Conducted experiments on 3 kg of LIBs from discarded laptop batteries (Dell and HP).
Recovered lithium and cobalt as oxalates with efficiencies of 90% and 85%, respectively.
Separated copper, aluminum, and graphite through mechanical and thermal processes.
assumptions: The use of biodegradable acids is more environmentally friendly than conventional inorganic acids.

gap_signals.text: The economic feasibility of the process on a larger scale is not discussed.
The process was tested on a small scale (3 kg of LIBs), and further work is needed to validate its scalability.

datasets_or_materials: 3 kg of LIBs from discarded Dell and HP laptop batteries

important_claims.claim: The process achieved high selectivity and reduced environmental impact.
important_claims.evidence: High recovery efficiencies of 90% for lithium and 85% for cobalt, and the use of biodegradable acids.

important_claims.claim: The process demonstrates a circular upcycling approach.
important_claims.evidence: Recovered materials were transformed into functional products such as aluminum keychains, copper jewelry, and graphite-based pencils.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a detailed methodology for the sustainable recovery of metals from LIBs using green solvents, which can be adapted and scaled by other researchers and industries working on battery recycling and resource valorization.

keynotes.title: Advances in Recycling and Resource Recovery of Post-Consumer Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) Waste for Sustainable Waste Management and Circular Economy

keynote.title: Advances in Recycling and Resource Recovery of Post-Consumer Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) Waste for Sustainable Waste Management and Circular Economy
keynote.core_problem: The extensive consumption of PET has led to significant environmental challenges due to post-consumer waste accumulation.
contributions: Explores recent advancements in PET recycling, focusing on chemical and enzymatic methods as sustainable alternatives to traditional mechanical recycling.
methods: Chemical recycling processes (glycolysis, methanolysis, and hydrolysis)
Enzymatic recycling using PET-degrading enzymes
limitations: Challenges in economic feasibility and large-scale implementation, including contamination and cost-effectiveness

gap_signals.text: Challenges in economic feasibility and large-scale implementation, including contamination and cost-effectiveness

gap_signals.signal_type: Need for further innovation and policy support
gap_signals.text: further innovation and policy support are essential to optimize these recycling strategies, foster a circular economy, and mitigate PET-related environmental pollution.

important_claims.claim: Chemical recycling processes break PET into monomers, enabling the production of high-quality recycled materials.
important_claims.evidence: Chemical recycling processes, including glycolysis, methanolysis, and hydrolysis, break PET into monomers, enabling the production of high-quality recycled materials.

important_claims.claim: Enzymatic recycling offers an eco-friendly approach with mild reaction conditions and high specificity.
important_claims.evidence: Enzymatic recycling, utilizing PET-degrading enzymes, offers an eco-friendly approach with mild reaction conditions and high specificity.

important_claims.claim: Enzymatic recycling has a lower environmental impact compared to other methods.
important_claims.evidence: The review compares these methods in terms of efficiency, scalability, energy requirements, and life cycle assessments (LCAs), highlighting enzymatic recycling’s lower environmental impact.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive comparison of chemical and enzymatic recycling methods for PET, which can be used as a foundation for future research in optimizing recycling strategies and developing more sustainable waste management practices.

keynotes.title: A review on recent environmental electrochemistry approaches for the consolidation of a circular economy model

keynote.title: A review on recent environmental electrochemistry approaches for the consolidation of a circular economy model
keynote.core_problem: The need to improve the efficiency, cost, and sustainability of chemical processes, including the extraction, use, recovery, and recycling of materials, to support a circular economy.
contributions: Summarizes recent advancements in environmental electrochemistry and bioelectrochemistry for material recovery and recycling.
assumptions: Electrochemical processes can significantly contribute to the reduction of solid waste and environmental impact in the chemical industry.

gap_signals.text: New process design or the optimization of the current technologies is a major necessity to enhance production and minimize the use of raw materials along with less generation of wastes and secondary by-products.

important_claims.claim: Electrochemistry is a growing field that can help reduce solid waste and the impact of chemical processes on the environment.
important_claims.evidence: Several electrochemical studies have benefited the recovery of important chemical compounds and elements through various processes like electrodeposition, electrowinning, electrocoagulation, and electrodialysis.

important_claims.claim: Bioelectrochemistry (using living organisms and microorganisms) is also gaining attention for 'mining' essential chemical elements.
important_claims.evidence: The text mentions the role of bioelectrochemistry in recovering and reusing chemical elements, contributing to a more sustainable and circular approach in the chemical industry.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how electrochemical and bioelectrochemical methods can be applied to support a circular economy, highlighting key areas for future research and development.

keynotes.title: Advancements and Challenges in Lithium-Ion Battery Lifecycle Management Toward a Sustainable Circular Economy for Electric Vehicles

keynote.title: Advancements and Challenges in Lithium-Ion Battery Lifecycle Management Toward a Sustainable Circular Economy for Electric Vehicles
keynote.core_problem: The increasing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) due to the global shift towards electric vehicles (EVs) poses significant challenges related to battery performance, sustainability, and end-of-life (EOL) management.
contributions: Comprehensive review of advancements in lithium-ion battery technologies
Analysis of degradation mechanisms and environmental impacts of battery disposal
Examination of challenges in EV battery recycling including safety, disassembly, and economic feasibility
Exploration of second-life applications for retired EV batteries in energy storage systems, microgrids, and hybrid renewable energy solutions
Proposal of advanced screening, refurbishment, and material recovery techniques to improve resource efficiency

gap_signals.text: Advanced screening, refurbishment, and material recovery techniques need further development to improve resource efficiency and reduce the need for new raw material extraction.

important_claims.claim: Second-life applications of retired EV batteries can extend battery usability while minimizing waste.
important_claims.evidence: The study explores the potential of using retired EV batteries in energy storage systems, microgrids, and hybrid renewable energy solutions.

important_claims.claim: A systematic framework for sustainable battery lifecycle management can promote a circular economy and optimize battery reusability.
important_claims.evidence: The research proposes such a framework to support the transition towards a cleaner, more sustainable future in electromobility.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a valuable overview of the current state and future directions in lithium-ion battery lifecycle management, which can serve as a foundation for researchers and policymakers to develop more sustainable and efficient strategies for managing EV batteries.

keynotes.title: An economic analysis of residential photovoltaic systems with lithium ion battery storage in the United States

keynote.title: An economic analysis of residential photovoltaic systems with lithium ion battery storage in the United States
keynote.core_problem: The economic viability of residential photovoltaic (PV) systems, especially when combined with lithium-ion battery storage, and their competitiveness with grid electricity.
contributions: Modeling the lifetime performance and economics of residential PV systems with lithium-ion batteries across all 50 U.S. states.
Investigating how PV size and battery capacity affect performance and cost metrics in California, Georgia, and Massachusetts.
methods: Economic modeling of PV-battery systems
Analysis of performance and cost metrics
experiments_or_evidence: Demonstration that appropriately sized PV-battery systems can be more affordable and potentially competitive with grid prices under certain conditions.
assumptions: Use of the federal investment tax credit and favorable financing terms.

gap_signals.text: No explicit limitation extracted, but further research could explore the impact of varying financing terms and other incentives on the economic viability of PV-battery systems.

datasets_or_materials: Data from 50 U.S. states regarding PV and battery costs, energy pricing, and related policies.

important_claims.claim: Appropriately sized PV-battery systems can be more affordable than PV systems alone.
important_claims.evidence: Modeling results across multiple states, particularly detailed in California, Georgia, and Massachusetts.

important_claims.claim: PV-battery systems may be competitive with grid prices under certain financial conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Calculated sell-back price required for bi-directional metering to reach cost parity with PV-battery systems in every state.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The economic models and findings can be used by researchers and policymakers to evaluate the feasibility and benefits of adopting PV-battery systems in different regions, considering local energy policies and market conditions.

keynotes.title: Advanced Air Mobility: Opportunities and Challenges Deploying eVTOLs for Air Ambulance Service

keynote.title: Advanced Air Mobility: Opportunities and Challenges Deploying eVTOLs for Air Ambulance Service
keynote.core_problem: Evaluating the operational and market viability of using eVTOL aircraft for air ambulance services, including the challenges and potential improvements needed to make them more reliable and cost-effective.
contributions: A five-step multi-method approach to analyze the potential of eVTOLs in the air ambulance market.
Comparison of eVTOL, hybrid VTOL, and rotorcraft for aeromedical applications.
methods: Preliminary scoping
Modeling performance metrics
Developing baseline assumptions
Analyzing scenarios
Applying a Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis
experiments_or_evidence: Analysis of various aircraft and propulsion types for aeromedical transport.
Evaluation of technological improvements such as reduced charge times, increased operational range, and battery swapping.

gap_signals.text: eVTOL aircraft could confront a number of operational and economic challenges for aeromedical applications compared to hybrid VTOL aircraft and rotorcraft.

important_claims.claim: eVTOL aircraft face significant operational and economic challenges for aeromedical applications.
important_claims.evidence: The study finds that technological improvements such as reduced charge times, increased operational range, and battery swapping could make the eVTOL aircraft more reliable and cost-effective for aeromedical transport.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The multi-method approach and detailed analysis can be used as a framework for evaluating other use cases of AAM, particularly in emergency response and aeromedical transport. The findings on the challenges and necessary improvements for eVTOLs can guide future research and development in this area.

keynotes.title: Battery revenues in energy and balancing markets for different battery technologies using FASBATT™

keynote.title: Battery revenues in energy and balancing markets for different battery technologies using FASBATT™
keynote.core_problem: The need for widespread installation of stationary electricity storage to provide power systems with more flexibility due to the increasing integration of renewable generation and the transition away from fossil-fuel generation, and the challenges in modeling and economic viability of different battery technologies.
contributions: A literature review and BESS industry analysis on the position of lithium-ion batteries, especially LFP chemistry, and other emerging battery technologies like vanadium redox flow, sodium-ion, and iron-air batteries.
Identification of the market opportunities and sustainability benefits of diversifying the battery market beyond lithium-ion technology.
methods: Literature review
BESS industry analysis
BESS simulations with different technology profiles
experiments_or_evidence: BESS simulations indicating that standalone BESS projects are not yet economically viable based on price arbitrage in the day-ahead spot market but have more attractive opportunities in the secondary frequency regulation market.
assumptions: Lithium-ion batteries, particularly LFP, are currently leading in stationary storage applications.
Emerging battery technologies could compete or coexist with LFP batteries in the future.
limitations: The market for non-lithium-ion battery technologies is still small and uncertain.
There is heterogeneity in the battery industry and uncertainty in the modeling of some battery behaviors, which complicates dispatch planning and economic analysis.

gap_signals.text: The market for non-lithium-ion battery technologies is still small and uncertain.
There is heterogeneity in the battery industry and uncertainty in the modeling of some battery behaviors, which complicates dispatch planning and economic analysis.
Improvements in BESS costs and performance are needed for standalone BESS projects to become economically feasible.
Continuous advances and the emergence of new battery technologies contribute to the difficulty in modeling and analyzing the economic viability of different business models.

important_claims.claim: Lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) chemistry is the primary technology for stationary storage applications.
important_claims.evidence: Aided by their dominance in portable electronics and electric vehicle markets.

important_claims.claim: Diversifying the battery market has potential benefits, including sustainability and cost-effectiveness.
important_claims.evidence: New technologies such as vanadium redox flow, sodium-ion, and iron-air batteries show promise.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future prospects of various battery technologies in stationary energy storage, highlighting the need for further research into cost reduction, performance improvement, and better modeling techniques for diverse battery chemistries.

keynotes.title: Techno-economics of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes for augmented reality displays and data communication applications

keynote.title: Techno-economics of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes for augmented reality displays and data communication applications
keynote.core_problem: Addressing the scalability and cost-effectiveness of molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) for high-volume manufacturing of axial nanowire light-emitting diodes (a-NWLEDs).
contributions: Introduction of MOCVD as a more scalable and cost-effective method for growing a-NWLEDs compared to MBE.
Comprehensive techno-economic analysis comparing MBE and MOCVD in terms of production cost and capital investment.
methods: Metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) for the growth of a-NWLEDs.
Techno-economic analysis comparing MBE and MOCVD.
experiments_or_evidence: Comparison of MicroLED wafer production cost and capital investment required to meet market demand between MBE and MOCVD.
assumptions: The performance of a-NWLEDs is comparable when grown using MBE or MOCVD.

gap_signals.text: No explicit limitation extracted.

important_claims.claim: MOCVD significantly outperforms MBE in both production cost and capital investment metrics.
important_claims.evidence: Results from the comprehensive techno-economic analysis.

important_claims.claim: MOCVD offers a viable pathway for meeting the performance and economic requirements of advanced display and communication applications.
important_claims.evidence: Demonstration of MOCVD's scalability and cost-effectiveness in the techno-economic analysis.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This study provides a foundation for further research into the industrial application and optimization of MOCVD for a-NWLEDs, particularly in the context of augmented reality displays and data communication.

keynotes.title: Techno-Economic Analysis of CO2 Storage in Depleted Oil and Gas Reservoirs, Saline Aquifers, and EOR

keynote.title: Techno-Economic Analysis of CO2 Storage in Depleted Oil and Gas Reservoirs, Saline Aquifers, and EOR
keynote.core_problem: Effective carbon capture and storage (CCS) strategies are needed to lower atmospheric CO2 levels, and there is a need to evaluate the feasibility, efficiency, and economic viability of different geological storage options for CO2.
contributions: Provides a techno-economic analysis of CO2 storage in depleted oil and gas reservoirs, saline aquifers, and EOR.
Evaluates the long-term impact of CO2 injection and the behavior of supercritical CO2 for estimating trapping mechanisms and sequestration efficiency.
Assesses the costs associated with CO2 capture, compression, transportation, injection, and monitoring, and the influence of policies, regulations, and market conditions on CCS project economics.
methods: Multidisciplinary methodology combining geological assessments, engineering principles, and economic models.
Economic evaluations conducted in compliance with Section 45Q tax credits.
experiments_or_evidence: The study uses economic analysis to compare the financial viability of different CO2 storage options, focusing on NPVs and cost variations influenced by various factors such as CO2 capture, transportation, and storage technologies, and financial incentives.
assumptions: All storing options (depleted oil and gas reservoirs, saline aquifers, and EOR) are capable of storing the desired CO2 volume (1 MM metric tons).
limitations: The economic superiority of depleted oil and gas reservoirs is based on ignoring the storage capacity, which may not be a practical assumption in all cases.

gap_signals.text: The economic superiority of depleted oil and gas reservoirs is based on ignoring the storage capacity, which may not be a practical assumption in all cases.

While the study highlights the economic benefits of using depleted oil and gas reservoirs, further research could explore the technical and environmental limitations of these storage options, especially when storage capacity is a limiting factor.

important_claims.claim: Depleted oil and gas reservoirs exhibit the highest financial viability for CO2 storage, particularly when used in conjunction with Direct Air Capture (DAC) technologies.
important_claims.evidence: Compliant DAC facilities utilizing depleted oil and gas reservoirs achieve NPVs upwards of $27 million, making them economically superior compared to saline aquifers and EOR if uniform stored volume is considered.

important_claims.claim: Saline aquifers and EOR present viable options due to higher storing capacity, but their financial performance is generally lower compared to depleted oil and gas reservoirs.
important_claims.evidence: The results show that while saline aquifers and EOR have higher storage capacities, their NPVs and overall financial performance are lower than those of depleted oil and gas reservoirs when the same volume of CO2 is stored.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The study provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating the techno-economic aspects of CO2 storage, which can be applied to other regions or storage scenarios. The detailed cost analysis and consideration of regulatory and market influences offer valuable insights for future CCS projects.

keynotes.title: Preventing Crossover in Redox Flow Batteries through Active Material Oligomerization

keynote.title: Preventing Crossover in Redox Flow Batteries through Active Material Oligomerization
keynote.core_problem: Crossover of active species between the positive and negative electrolyte solutions in redox flow batteries, leading to capacity losses.
contributions: Development of tris(dialkylamino)cyclopropenium oligomers as a new posolyte (catholyte) material.
Use of polymers with intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) as membranes to limit crossover.
methods: Synthesis and characterization of tris(dialkylamino)cyclopropenium oligomers.
Evaluation of the performance of these oligomers with size exclusion membranes composed of PIMs.
experiments_or_evidence: Synthesis of oligomers and their evaluation with PIM membranes.
assumptions: Increasing the molecular weight of the oligomers and decreasing membrane pore size will minimize crossover.

gap_signals.text: Membranes for nonaqueous and other pH conditions are still in development.

important_claims.claim: The combination of an oligomeric posolyte with PIM membranes can minimize crossover in redox flow batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Demonstration of a dual approach using oligomeric posolyte and PIMs to minimize crossover in redox flow batteries.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings provide a novel approach to reduce crossover in redox flow batteries, which could lead to more efficient and commercially viable battery systems, particularly for grid storage applications.

boxue_delegation_plans.goal: 不要泛泛讨论‘储能技术的未来’。围绕一个可证伪的科学问题：'电极材料（如钴酸锂、磷酸铁锂、硫正极）的固有化学稳定性、电解液分解电位、锂/钠离子迁移动力学以及固态电解质界面（SEI）的演化，是否共同决定了一个明确的能量密度上限（例如 < 500 Wh/kg）和循环寿命阈值（例如 < 10,000次，在80%容量保持率下），从而在技术和经济可行性上构成固态/液态电池的固有极限；该上限是否可以通过全新的化学体系（如锂-硫、锂-空气、钠离子）或梯度浓度/纳米结构工程被实质性突破？'

comparison.prompt_assign_task: implemented as persistent create_task DAG entries with assigned agent, phase, dependency, deliverable, priority, and acceptance criteria
comparison.prompt_review_output: implemented as reviewer_gate plus boxue_final dependency gates; Boxue can add revision tasks via the task system
comparison.prompt_synthesize: implemented as bianlun_synthesis, paperwriter_draft, and boxue_final synthesis tasks
comparison.prompt_adjust_plan: implemented operationally by creating additional tasks or new delegation plans after reviewing outputs
comparison.prompt_finalize: implemented as boxue_final decision task, not as specialist execution

stronger_than_old_pipeline_tasks: role-specific tasks instead of generic phase tasks
risk constraints embedded in every task
optional teammate spawning for unblocked specialist work
prompt_alignment.remaining_manual_gate: Boxue still needs to review outputs and decide whether to create revision tasks; this preserves human/lead control over shared project state.

coordination_policy.boxue_role: decompose, assign, review, synthesize, adjust, finalize
coordination_policy.specialist_role: execute only the assigned scientific subtask
coordination_policy.shared_state: state-changing PaperGraph/project updates should be gated by lead/synthesis tasks

tasks.title: Build grounded PaperGraph evidence substrate
acceptance_criteria: At least one verifiable source or an explicit retrieval-failure report
Method/scenario/benchmark/contribution/limitation fields are populated or flagged unknown
Unsupported claims are marked for human review

tasks.title: Rank high-value knowledge gaps
acceptance_criteria: Every reported gap has supporting references or is flagged as ungrounded

tasks.title: Generate and evolve gap-grounded hypotheses
acceptance_criteria: Each hypothesis links to a gap_id
Each hypothesis contains mechanism and falsification condition

tasks.title: Run Socratic critique
acceptance_criteria: At least one counterexample per hypothesis
At least one alternative explanation per hypothesis

tasks.title: Moderate structured debate and synthesize refined hypothesis
acceptance_criteria: Arguments are separated into factual vs conceptual disagreements
Refined hypothesis lists improvements from critique

boxue_delegation_plans.next_step: Let unblocked specialist tasks run first. Boxue should review outputs at dependency gates, then adjust or unlock downstream phases.

discussed the recent progress in the development of air-stable, electrochemically stable, and cost-effective NaxTMO2, emphasizing the favorable redox-active cations, working mechanisms, and techno-economics
irreversible phase transformations during cycling, poor air stability, complex charge-compensation mechanisms, and relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs
Full text excerpt:
Sodium-ion batteries (NIBs), which possess a similar cell configuration and working mechanism, have already been proven as ideal alternatives for large-scale energy storage systems.

Sodium layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2, TM = transition metal/s), such as Mn-based sodium layered oxides, represent an important family of cathode materials with the potential to reduce costs, increase energy density and cycling stability, and improve the safety of NIBs for large-scale ener...[truncated]

Our work has focused on the techno-economic analysis, the degradation mechanism of NaxTMO2 upon cycling and air exposure, and the development of effective strategies to improve their electrochemical performances and air stability.

references: R. Areola et al. (2025) Integrated Energy Storage Systems for Enhanced Grid Efficiency: A Comprehensive Review of Technologies and Applications doi:10.3390/en18071848

evidence_type_annotations.claim: Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework

a comprehensive review and framework for deploying Integrated Energy Storage Systems (IESSs) to enhance grid efficiency and stability, with a focus on hybrid storage systems and regionally tailored strategies
reliance on static cost projections and evolving regulatory landscapes
Full text excerpt:
This study presents a comprehensive review and framework for deploying Integrated Energy Storage Systems (IESSs) to enhance grid efficiency and stability.

By leveraging a Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework, this study synthesizes techno-economic optimization, lifecycle emissions, and policy frameworks to evaluate storage technologies such as lithium-ion batteries, pumped hydro storage, and vanadium flow batteries.

The framework prioritizes hybrid storage systems (e.g., battery–supercapacitor configurations), demonstrating 15% higher grid stability in high-renewable penetration scenarios, and validates findings through global case studies, including the Hornsdale Power Reserve (90–95% round-trip efficiency) an...[truncated]

Despite reliance on static cost projections and evolving regulatory landscapes, the MCDA framework’s dynamic adaptation mechanisms, including sensitivity analysis for carbon taxes (USD 100/ton CO2-eq boosts hydrogen viability by 25%), ensure scalability across diverse grids.

Full text excerpt: a comprehensive review and framework for deploying Integrated Energy Storage Systems (IESSs) to enhance grid efficiency and stability, with a focus on hybrid storage systems and regionally tailored strategies reliance on static cost projections and evolving regulatory landscapes
